epigraph.text: But solid food is for the mature, for those who have their powers of discernment trained by constant practice.
epigraph.quote: To classify singularities is to understand the local behavior of functions.

content.text:
The principal part of a Laurent series tells us what kind of singularity 
a function has. We classify singularities into three types.

content.title: Isolated Singularity
content.content:
A point $z_0$ is an **isolated singularity** 
of $f$ if $f$ is analytic in some punctured disk 
$0 < |z - z_0| < R$ but not at $z_0$ itself.

content.title: Classification of Isolated Singularities
content.content:
Let $z_0$ be an isolated singularity of $f$ with Laurent series 
$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n(z-z_0)^n$. Then $z_0$ is:

1. A **removable singularity** if 
 $a_n = 0$ for all $n < 0$. The principal part is zero.

2. A **pole** of **order** $m$ if $a_{-m} \neq 0$ 
 but $a_n = 0$ for $n < -m$. The principal part is finite.

3. An **essential singularity** if 
 $a_n \neq 0$ for infinitely many $n < 0$. The principal part is infinite.

content.title: Removable Singularities
content.content:
The following are equivalent for an isolated singularity $z_0$:

1. $z_0$ is removable.

2. $\lim_{z \to z_0} f(z)$ exists (and is finite).

3. $f$ is bounded near $z_0$.

4. $(z - z_0)f(z) \to 0$ as $z \to z_0$.
If any holds, defining $f(z_0) = \lim_{z \to z_0} f(z)$ makes $f$ analytic.
content.label: Theorem

content.problem:
$f(z) = (\sin z)/z$ has a removable singularity at $z = 0$ since 
$\lim_{z \to 0}(\sin z)/z = 1$.

content.title: Poles
content.content:
The following are equivalent:

1. $z_0$ is a pole of order $m$.

2. $\lim_{z \to z_0} |f(z)| = \infty$.

3. $f(z) = g(z)/(z-z_0)^m$ where $g$ is analytic at $z_0$ with $g(z_0) \neq 0$.

4. $(z-z_0)^m f(z)$ has a removable singularity at $z_0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Casorati-Weierstrass Theorem
content.content:
If $z_0$ is an essential singularity of $f$, then for every $w \in \C$ 
and every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $z$ with $0 < |z - z_0| < \epsilon$ 
such that $|f(z) - w| < \epsilon$. In other words, $f$ comes arbitrarily 
close to every value near an essential singularity.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The stronger **Picard's Theorem** says $f$ 
actually *attains* every value (with at most one exception) 
infinitely often near an essential singularity.

content.problem:
$f(z) = e^{1/z}$ has an essential singularity at $z = 0$. Its Laurent 
series is $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!z^n}$, with infinitely many 
negative powers.

content.text: To study $f$ near $\infty$, we examine $g(w) = f(1/w)$ near $w = 0$.

content.title: Singularity at Infinity
content.content:
We say $f$ has a removable singularity, pole, or essential singularity 
at $\infty$ according to the type of singularity $g(w) = f(1/w)$ has at 
$w = 0$.

content.problem:
$f(z) = z^2$ has a pole of order 2 at $\infty$ since $g(w) = 1/w^2$ has 
a pole of order 2 at $w = 0$.

marginNotes.text:
My grace is sufficient for you, for 
my power is made perfect in weakness.

marginNotes.author: Karl Weierstrass
marginNotes.text:
The character of a singularity is revealed 
by the principal part.
